Dependency Chain Generation: # engine = VisualizerEngine(watsonx_client) # result = await engine.generate_dependency_chain("/path/to/project", max_depth=4) # print(result) # 2. Feature Flow Generation: # engine = VisualizerEngine(watsonx_client) # result = await engine.generate_feature_flow("/path/to/project", feature_name="User Authentication") # print(result) # 3. Project Concept Generation: # engine = VisualizerEngine(watsonx_client) # result = await engine.generate_project_concept("/path/to/project") # print(result) ## Quick Start Guide the Builder 🤖 ``` --- ## 🚀 Quick‑Start Guide for the **Visualizer Engine** ### 1️⃣ Minimal Setup | Step | What to do | Why | |------|-------------|-----| | **A. Install dependencies** | ```bash
pip install aiohttp pydantic mermaid-cli``` | The engine talks to WatsonX (aiohttp), parses Python (built‑in), and renders Mermaid diagrams (pydantic for schema, mcli for PNG output). | | **B. Get a WatsonX client** | ```python
from watsonx_sdk import WatsonXClient
wx = WatsonXClient(api_key="YOUR_API_KEY", base_url="https://api.watsonx.ibm.com")``` | The engine sends natural‑language prompts to WatsonX for the heavy‑lifting of feature‑flow and concept analysis. | | **C. Create a Visualizer instance** | ```python
engine = VisualizerEngine(wx)``` | This is the entry point – it bundles all generators. | | **D. (Optional) Configure output folder** | ```python
output_dir = Path("./visuals")``` | All diagrams will be saved here unless you pass a custom path. | > **Tip:** If you don’t have a WatsonX API key yet, sign up at https://ibm.com/watsonx and create a free‑tier key. --- ### 2️⃣ Basic Usage Example ```python # demo.py import asyncio from visualizer_engine import VisualizerEngine # <-- import the class from the file you saved async def main(): # 1️⃣ WatsonX client wx = WatsonXClient(api_key="YOUR_API_KEY") # 2️⃣ Visualizer engine engine = VisualizerEngine(wx) # 3️⃣ Generate a Dependency Chain diagram result = await engine.generate_dependency_chain( project_path="/path/to/your/project", # <-- change to your repo output_path=None, # optional – leave None to auto‑name max_depth=3, include_external=True ) print("✅ Dependency chain result:", result) # 4️⃣ Generate a Feature Flow diagram (async) feature_result = await engine.generate_feature_flow( project_path="/path/to/your/project", feature_name="User Registration" # optional – let it infer if omitted ) print("✅ Feature flow result:", feature_result) # 5️⃣ Generate a Project Concept diagram concept_result = await engine.generate_project_concept( project_path="/path/to/your/project", output_path=None ) print("✅ Project concept result:", concept_result) # Run the async main() if __name__ == "__main__": asyncio.run(main()) ``` **What you’ll see** * `dependency-chain_2025-08-21_14-30-00.md` – a Markdown file containing a **Mermaid** graph and a short table of modules. * `feature-flow_User_Registration_2025-08-21_14-30-01.md` – similar, but a **sequence diagram** of the chosen feature. * `project-concept_AppCore_2025-08-21_14-30-02.md` – a **conceptual architecture** diagram plus a short description of the project. All PNG images are saved next to the Markdown files (e.g., `dependency-chain_2025-08-21_14-30-00.png`). --- ### 3️⃣ Next Steps & Extensibility | Goal | How to achieve it | |------|-------------------| | **Add a new diagram type** (e.g., “Data Flow”) | 1. Implement a new async method in `VisualizerEngine` (e.g., `generate_data_flow`).
2. Write a matching `_analyze_*_with_ai` helper that talks to WatsonX.
3. Create a `_create_*_mermaid` renderer and a `_format_*_output` formatter.
4. Add the new diagram type to the `save_mermaid_as_png` call. | | **Swap the LLM provider** (e.g., OpenAI, Claude) | Replace the `watsonx` calls with your preferred client, keeping the same prompt‑building helpers (`build_feature_flow_prompt`, `build_project_concept_prompt`). | | **Cache WatsonX responses** | Wrap `_analyze_*_with_ai` with an LRU cache (`functools.lru_cache`) keyed by `(project_path, feature_name)` to avoid re‑sending identical prompts. | | **Support other languages** (Java, Go, TypeScript) | 1. Add a language detector (`detect_language`).
2. Extend `_analyze_dependencies` to run language‑specific parsers (e.g., `javalang` for Java).
3. Adjust the prompt templates to mention the new language. | | **Run in a Docker container** | Dockerfile example:
```dockerfile
FROM python:3.12-slim
WORKDIR /app
COPY . .
RUN pip install -r requirements.txt
CMD ["python", "demo.py"]```
Mount your project folder as a volume and run `docker run -v $(pwd):/app your_image`. | | **Add CI‑integrated visual checks** | In a GitHub Action, call the engine after each PR merge and upload the generated PNGs as artifacts. | --- ### 4️⃣ Links to Detailed Documentation | Topic | Documentation | |-------|----------------| | **WatsonX SDK** (Python) | https://ibm.github.io/watsonx-sdk-python/ | | **Mermaid rendering (pydantic & mcli)** | https://mermaid-js.github.io/mermaid-cli/ | | **Python AST parsing** | https://docs.python.org/3/library/ast.html | | **Mermaid syntax reference** | https://mermaid-js.github.io/mermaid/#/ | | **Project structure analysis best‑practices** | https://realpython.com/python-project-layout/ | Feel free to dive into the code – each class method is heavily commented, and the prompt‑building helpers (`build_feature_flow_prompt`, `build_project_concept_prompt`) are deliberately isolated for easy maintenance. --- ### 🎉 You’re ready! Run the demo script, point it at any Python codebase, and you’ll instantly get **visual, shareable diagrams** that help new team members onboard faster. If you hit any snags, the detailed docs above (or the inline comments) will guide you through the customization process. Happy visualizing! 🚀 ## User Manual the Builder 🤖 # <3 ``` # Visualizer Engine - Core Orchestrator ## Overview and Purpose The Visualizer Engine is a production-grade visualization generator designed for project onboarding. It automatically generates visual diagrams to aid in understanding project structure, dependencies, feature flows, and overall system concepts. The engine leverages AI to analyze project contexts and generate meaningful visual representations using Mermaid syntax, which can then be rendered as PNG images. ## Installation Instructions 1. **Clone the Repository**: Ensure you have the repository containing the Visualizer Engine code. 2. **Set Up Environment**: Create a virtual environment and install the required dependencies listed in the repository's `requirements.txt` file. 3. **Configure Watsonx Client**: Set up your Watsonx client credentials and initialize the client in your code. 4. **Run the Engine**: Execute the Visualizer Engine script with the appropriate parameters for your project. ## Configuration Options - **`project_path`**: The path to the project directory you want to visualize. - **`output_path`**: The directory or file path where the generated diagrams will be saved. If not specified, diagrams will be saved in the default location. - **`max_depth`**: The maximum depth for dependency chain analysis (default is 3). - **`include_external`**: A boolean flag to include external dependencies in the dependency chain analysis (default is `False`). - **`feature_name`**: The name of a specific feature to analyze in the feature flow mapping. ## Usage Examples ### Generating Dependency Chain Diagram ```python engine = VisualizerEngine(watsonx_client) result = await engine.generate_dependency_chain( project_path='/path/to/project', output_path='/path/to/output', max_depth=3, include_external=True ) print(result) ``` ### Generating Feature Flow Diagram ```python engine = VisualizerEngine(watsonx_client) result = await engine.generate_feature_flow( project_path='/path/to/project', feature_name='User Authentication', output_path='/path/to/output' ) print(result) ``` ### Generating Project Concept Diagram ```python engine = VisualizerEngine(watsonx_client) result = await engine.generate_project_concept( project_path='/path/to/project', output_path='/path/to/output' ) print(result) ``` ## Common Use Cases - **Project Onboarding**: Quickly generate visual diagrams to help new team members understand the project structure and dependencies. - **System Architecture Documentation**: Create comprehensive diagrams to document the system architecture and feature flows. - **Dependency Management**: Analyze and visualize dependencies to identify potential issues and optimize the project structure. ## Best Practices - **Regular Updates**: Regularly update the Visualizer Engine to benefit from the latest features and optimizations. - **Customize Prompts**: Tailor the prompts used by the AI to better fit your project's specific needs and terminology. - **Review Generated Diagrams**: Manually review the generated diagrams to ensure accuracy and make necessary adjustments. - **Integrate with CI/CD**: Integrate the Visualizer Engine into your CI/CD pipeline to automatically generate and update diagrams as the project evolves. ## How-To Guide the Builder 🤖 """ Objective: The Visualizer Engine is a production-grade visualization generator designed for project onboarding. It auto-generates visual diagrams, such as dependency chain diagrams, feature flow maps, and project concept visualizations, based on the provided project path and optional output path. The engine utilizes AI to analyze the project structure and generate accurate and informative diagrams. Prerequisites: - Python 3.x - Required libraries: ast, re, json, pathlib, typing, collections, lib.utils, lib.visualizer.prompts, lib.visualizer.renderers - Watsonx client for AI analysis Step-by-Step Instructions: 1. Import the necessary libraries and modules: - ast - re - json - pathlib - typing (Dict, Any, List, Optional, Set) - collections (defaultdict) - lib.utils (detect_language, read_file_safe, get_timestamp) - lib.visualizer.prompts (build_feature_flow_prompt, build_project_concept_prompt) - lib.visualizer.renderers (save_mermaid_as_png, DiagramGenerationError) 2. Create an instance of the VisualizerEngine class by providing a Watsonx client: ```python watsonx_client = WatsonxClient() visualizer_engine = VisualizerEngine(watsonx_client) ``` 3. Generate a dependency chain diagram: ```python project_path = "/path/to/project" output_path = "/path/to/output/directory" result = visualizer_engine.generate_dependency_chain(project_path, output_path) ``` - `project_path`: The path to the project directory. - `output_path` (optional): The path to the output directory where the generated diagram will be saved. - `max_depth` (optional): The maximum depth of the dependency chain (default is 3). - `include_external` (optional): Whether to include external dependencies (default is False). 4. Generate a feature flow map: ```python project_path = "/path/to/project" feature_name = "Feature Name" output_path = "/path/to/output/directory" result = visualizer_engine.generate_feature_flow(project_path, feature_name, output_path) ``` - `project_path`: The path to the project directory. - `feature_name` (optional): The name of the feature to analyze. - `output_path` (optional): The path to the output directory where the generated diagram will be saved. 5. Generate a project concept visualization: ```python project_path = "/path/to/project" output_path = "/path/to/output/directory" result = visualizer_engine.generate_project_concept(project_path, output_path) ``` - `project_path`: The path to the project directory. - `output_path` (optional): The path to the output directory where the generated diagram will be saved. Expected Outcomes: - The `generate_dependency_chain` method returns a dictionary containing the following keys: - `success`: Indicates whether the generation was successful (True) or not (False). - `diagram_type`: The type of diagram generated ("dependency_chain"). - `markdown`: The generated markdown content of the diagram. - `mermaid`: The Mermaid code representing the diagram. - `saved_to`: The path where the generated diagram is saved (if `output_path` is provided). - `stats`: A dictionary containing statistics about the generated diagram, such as the total number of modules, total dependencies, and external dependencies. - The `generate_feature_flow` method returns a dictionary containing the following keys: - `success`: Indicates whether the generation was successful (True) or not (False). - `diagram_type`: The type of diagram generated ("feature_flow"). - `markdown`: The generated markdown content of the diagram. - `mermaid`: The Mermaid code representing the diagram. - `saved_to`: The path where the generated diagram is saved (if `output_path` is provided). - `features_analyzed`: The number of features analyzed. - The `generate_project_concept` method returns a dictionary containing the following keys: - `success`: Indicates whether the generation was successful (True) or not (False). - `diagram_type`: The type of diagram generated ("project_concept"). - `markdown`: The generated markdown content of the diagram. - `mermaid`: The Mermaid code representing the diagram. - `saved_to`: The path where the generated diagram is saved (if `output_path` is provided). - `image_saved_to`: The path where the generated PNG image is saved (if `output_path` is provided). - `components`: The number of components in the project concept. Tips and Warnings: - Ensure that the provided project path exists and is accessible. - If the `output_path` is not provided, the generated diagrams will not be saved to a file. - The `max_depth` parameter in `generate_dependency_chain` controls the depth of the dependency chain. Adjust it according to your project's complexity. - The `include_external` parameter in `generate_dependency_chain` determines whether to include external dependencies in the diagram. Set it to True if you want to include them. - The `feature_name` parameter in `generate_feature_flow` allows you to specify a particular feature to analyze. If not provided, the engine will analyze all features. - The generated diagrams are saved as Markdown files by default. If you want to save them as PNG images, you can use the `save_mermaid_as_png` function from the `lib.visualizer.renderers` module. - Handle any exceptions that may occur during the generation process and provide appropriate error messages or fallback mechanisms. - Customize the prompts and rendering logic in the `lib.visualizer.prompts` and `lib.visualizer.renderers` modules to match your specific project requirements and styling preferences. ## Tutorial the Builder 🤖 # Feel free to refactor or extend with new visualizations! # This engine is now ready for modularization of analysis components. # ------------------------------------------------------------------ # # UNIT TESTING FRAMEWORK # # ------------------------------------------------------------------ # import unittest from unittest.mock import MagicMock, patch from pathlib import Path class VisualizerEngineTests(unittest.TestCase): def setUp(self): self.fake_watsonx = MagicMock() self.engine = VisualizerEngine(self.fake_watsonx) def test_get_file_content(self): # Test normal file read self.assertEqual(self.engine._get_file_content("test.txt"), "content") # Test tuple return handling self.assertEqual(self.engine._get_file_content("test2.txt"), "") @patch('lib.visualizer.renderers.save_mermaid_as_png') def test_dependency_chain(self, mock_save_png): mock_save_png.return_value = "path/to/png" result = self.engine.generate_dependency_chain("test_project") self.assertIn("diagram_type", result) self.assertIn("markdown", result) self.assertIn("mermaid", result) @patch('lib.visualizer.renderers.save_mermaid_as_png') def test_feature_flow(self, mock_save_png): result = self.engine.generate_feature_flow("test_project") self.assertIn("diagram_type", result) self.assertIn("markdown", result) self.assertIn("mermaid", result) @patch('lib.visualizer.renderers.save_mermaid_as_png') def test_project_concept(self, mock_save_png): result = self.engine.generate_project_concept("test_project") self.assertIn("diagram_type", result) self.assertIn("markdown", result) self.assertIn("mermaid", result) if __name__ == '__main__': unittest.main() # ------------------------------------------------------------------ # # PRACTICE EXERCISES # # ------------------------------------------------------------------ # # Exercise 1: Dependency Chain Exploration # 1. Create a small sample project with 3 modules (a.py, b.py, c.py) where: # - a.py imports b # - b.py imports c # 2. Run the dependency_chain generator on this project # 3. Verify the output shows the correct module relationships # 4. Add an external library import in a.py and re-run with include_external=True # 5. Confirm the external dependency appears in the diagram # Exercise 2: Feature Flow Mapping # 1. In your sample project, create a feature module (feature_x.py) with: # - A function that reads data from a database # - A function that processes the data # - A function that writes results to a file # 2. Run the feature_flow generator # 3. Check that the sequence diagram shows the correct flow between components # 4. Modify the feature to include an external API call and re-run # 5. Verify the external service appears in the diagram # Exercise 3: Project Concept Visualization # 1. Create a README.md in your sample project describing a fictional purpose # 2. Run the project_concept generator # 3. Check that the concept diagram includes a UI component and server components # 4. Add a database component to your project structure and re-run # 5. Verify the database component appears in the concept diagram # ------------------------------------------------------------------ # # SUMMARY AND NEXT STEPS # # ------------------------------------------------------------------ # # Learning Objectives Recap: # - Understand how to analyze project dependencies # - Learn to map feature flows using AI-assisted analysis # - Visualize overall project concepts with architectural diagrams # - Practice working with abstract syntax trees (AST) and regular expressions # - Integrate with external AI services for enhanced analysis # Next Steps: # 1. Experiment with different project structures to see how the diagrams adapt # 2. Try modifying the prompts in lib/visualizer/prompts.py to get different analysis results # 3. Explore the renderers in lib/visualizer/renderers.py to customize diagram outputs # 4. Consider adding more analysis components like: # - Code quality metrics visualizer # - Test coverage visualizer # - Security vulnerability scanner visualizer # 5. Integrate this engine into your CI/CD pipeline for automatic documentation generation # Remember, the best way to learn is by doing. Modify the code, break things, and fix them. # That's how you'll truly understand how this visualizer engine works and how to extend it for your needs. ``` ## Troubleshooting Guide the Builder 🚧 ``` ## Troubleshooting Guide ### Common Errors and Solutions | Error | Description | Solution | |-------|-------------|----------| | `ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'lib.utils'` | The `lib` directory is not in your Python path. | Ensure `lib` is in your `PYTHONPATH` or run the script from the project root. | | `ImportError: cannot import name 'DiagramGenerationError'` | The `DiagramGenerationError` class is missing from `lib.visualizer.renderers`. | Verify the `renderers.py` file contains the `DiagramGenerationError` class definition. | | `AttributeError: 'VisualizerEngine' object has no attribute 'watsonx'` | The `watsonx_client` argument is not passed to the `VisualizerEngine` constructor. | Instantiate `VisualizerEngine` with a valid `watsonx` client object. | | `TypeError: 'str' object is not callable` | `target_path.iterdir()` is being called as a function. | Ensure `target_path` is a `Path` object, not a string. | | `FileNotFoundError: [Errno 2] No such file or directory` | The specified file path does not exist. | Verify the file path is correct and the file exists. | ### Debugging Steps 1. **Check Python Environment** - Verify the Python version and installed packages. - Ensure the `lib` directory is in your Python path. 2. **Verify File Paths** - Confirm all file paths used in the code exist and are accessible. - Use `Path.resolve()` to get the absolute path of files. 3. **Inspect Variable Values** - Use print statements or a debugger to inspect variable values at different stages. - Check the contents of `result` in `_get_file_content` to ensure it returns the expected data. 4. **Handle Exceptions** - Wrap code blocks in `try-except` blocks to catch and log exceptions. - Print or log the exception message to identify the issue. 5. **Test Individual Components** - Isolate and test individual functions or methods to ensure they work as expected. - Use unit tests to verify the functionality of each component. ### FAQ | Question | Answer | |----------|--------| | **What is the purpose of the `VisualizerEngine` class?** | The `VisualizerEngine` class is designed to generate visual diagrams for project onboarding, including dependency chain maps, feature flow mappings, and project concept visualizations. | | **How do I instantiate the `VisualizerEngine` class?** | Instantiate the class by passing a valid `watsonx` client object: `engine = VisualizerEngine(watsonx_client)`. | | **What are the available methods in the `VisualizerEngine` class?** | The class provides methods to generate dependency chain diagrams (`generate_dependency_chain`), feature flow mappings (`generate_feature_flow`), and project concept visualizations (`generate_project_concept`). | | **How do I save the generated diagrams?** | Use the `output_path` parameter in the respective methods to specify the desired output path for the generated diagrams. | | **What are the supported diagram types?** | The supported diagram types are `dependency_chain`, `feature_flow`, and `project_concept`. | ### Known Issues | Issue | Description | Workaround | |--------|-------------|------------| | `DiagramGenerationError` not defined | The `DiagramGenerationError` class is missing from `lib.visualizer.renderers`. | Ensure the `DiagramGenerationError` class is properly defined in `lib.visualizer.renderers`. | | `watsonx` client not available | The `watsonx` client is not properly initialized or passed to the `VisualizerEngine`. | Verify the `watsonx` client is correctly initialized and passed to the `VisualizerEngine` constructor. | | `max_depth` parameter not respected | The `_analyze_dependencies` method does not limit the depth of the dependency analysis. | Modify the `_analyze_dependencies` method to enforce the `max_depth` parameter. | | `external` dependencies not included | External dependencies are not included in the dependency chain diagram. | Set `include_external=True` when calling `generate_dependency_chain` to include external dependencies. | | `async` methods not awaited | The `generate_feature_flow` method is not awaited, leading to incomplete execution. | Ensure the `generate_feature_flow` method is awaited when called: `await engine.generate_feature_flow(...)`. | ### Support Resources - **Documentation**: Refer to the project documentation for detailed information on the `VisualizerEngine` class and its methods. - **GitHub Repository**: Check the GitHub repository for the latest updates, issues, and contributions: [GitHub Repository](https://github.com/your-repo/visualizer-engine). - **Issue Tracker**: Report bugs or request features using the issue tracker on the GitHub repository. - **Community Forum**: Join the community forum to ask questions, share experiences, and get help from other users: [Community Forum](https://forum.your-project.com). - **Contact Support**: For further assistance, contact the support team via email at support@your-project.com or through the support portal. ## Requirements Specification the Builder 🛠️
